does: This paper introduces Uncertainty-Aware Reward Discounting (UARD), a framework that jointly models two sources of uncertainty in RLHF systems: epistemic uncertainty in value estimation through ensemble disagreement and aleatoric uncertainty in human preference annotations through annotator variability. The method combines these uncertainty signals via a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter that adaptively modulates reward weights during policy optimization. The authors prove that this dynamic discounting preserves the contraction property of the Bellman operator, ensuring convergence guarantees, and demonstrate empirically that UARD reduces reward hacking behavior compared to baseline RLHF approaches that treat uncertainty sources independently.
connects: This work builds on longstanding challenges in RLHF where learned reward models struggle with distribution shift and noisy preference data. It extends prior research addressing epistemic uncertainty through ensemble methods for exploration and research tackling preference inconsistency during reward model training. The paper specifically responds to the limitation that existing approaches treat uncertainty sources separately—using epistemic uncertainty for exploration while discarding preference uncertainty information during policy optimization. It combines insights from uncertainty quantification in machine learning with the RLHF alignment literature, drawing on Bayesian approaches to reward learning and recent work highlighting how policy optimization can exploit reward model errors.
enables: This framework opens directions for more robust alignment of language models and other systems trained via RLHF by providing a principled way to hedge against both known and unknown reward model failures. The theoretical convergence guarantees enable practitioners to use uncertainty-aware reward discounting with confidence that the learning dynamics remain stable. Future work could extend this approach to other sources of uncertainty in the reward specification pipeline, apply it to multi-objective reward settings where different humans have conflicting preferences, or develop adaptive filtering strategies that learn to weight uncertainty sources based on task characteristics. The method may also inspire similar uncertainty-aware modifications to other components of the RLHF pipeline, potentially improving alignment and reducing specification gaming more broadly.
